storytect.title: Storytect
storytect.subtitle: A desktop editor that unfolds a story as a codex and lets you weave it into your own worldbuilding
storytect.description:
A desktop app that unfolds an extracted story (standardized JSON) into a codex of wiki, relations graph, and timeline views, and lets writers design their own worldbuilding model across four layers: Galpi (class), fields, items, and Yeoeum (diagram). An MCP server exposes the same codex as a second entry point so an external AI can co-edit it alongside the user.

Built as a single-binary Tauri + Next.js app, with an automatic Rust ↔ TypeScript type generation pipeline using specta + tauri-specta. 195 of 219 commits were authored together with Claude through custom multi-agent orchestrators.

overview.introduction: Desktop app that unfolds extracted story data as a codex, letting users browse it across multiple views and design their own Galpi (categories) and Yeoeum (diagrams) to construct their worldbuilding
overview.features: Instant JSON visualization, user-defined OO editor, global TimeBar, MCP server for external AI co-editing

0.subtitle: Standardized JSON Interface + Extraction-Rendering Separation
0.caption: Manifest.json as the entry point for all data
0.0: Problem: When extraction logic is coupled with the visualization code, every visualization change risks shifting the extraction output format and breaking user data compatibility. The same coupling also closes the door to reusing the extraction skill with any other visualization tool.
0.1: Analyze: brief.md fixed extraction and visualization as fully separated stages, with the app trusting only the standardized JSON folder and reading from manifest.json as a single entry point. The separation lets the extraction skill be reused independent of the visualization tool, and lets the visualization layer assume a stable data format and be redesigned freely.
0.2: Action: The app was built to trust only the standardized JSON folder produced by the extraction skill, with manifest.json as the entry point listing slot inventory, metadata, and available_views in one file.
0.3: Result: Data compatibility held across the visualization-layer rewrites.

1.subtitle: Promoting the TimeBar to a Global Axis
1.caption: A single time axis shared by every view
1.0: Problem: When the time axis lives only inside the timeline view, every other view (relations, wiki) needs duplicated time-handling code to highlight the same moment, or the time context is lost when switching views.
1.1: Analyze: PLANNING_REVISE redefined time as a semantic axis rather than a layout shape, and made it a principle that any time field auto-registers itself onto the TimeBar. Pulling the TimeBar out of view modes and placing it as a single component above every canvas also dovetails naturally with the user-defined OO model. The trade-off is that every item must carry a time field.
1.2: Action: Promoted the existing time axis from inside the timeline view to a global component. The TimeBar sits pinned at the top of every view, and dragging it highlights items present at that point while fading the rest.
1.3: Result: Consistent moment-highlighting works across every view including relations and wiki. The abstraction survived both visualization rewrites untouched.

2.subtitle: User-Defined 4-Layer Object-Oriented Model (PLANNING_OO)
2.caption: Template / Class / Field / Instance
2.0: Problem: Listing 13 views drifted away from the single picture worldbuilders carried in their heads, and adding more views did not close the gap.
2.1: Analyze: PLANNING_OO verified up-front via a 13-view → 6-template mapping table (Graph / Timeline / Matrix / Curve / List / Tree) that user-defined territory could expand without losing expressive power. Moving slot type declarations from code into user metadata unifies characters, events, and relations under one data shape, so meta-driven validation and inline wiki relation editing land naturally on the same structure.
2.2: Action: The OOP model was lifted into the editor as a four-layer structure of Galpi / fields / items / Yeoeum (PLANNING_OO.md). Relations were treated as items so that characters, events, and relationships share the same data shape, and SlotMeta.class_meta moved slot type declarations into user metadata.
2.3: Result: The slot type declaration UI, meta-driven validation, and inline relation editing in the wiki accumulated on top of the same structure.

3.subtitle: Blank Canvas + Friendly-Language Label Dictionary (PLANNING_REVISE)
3.caption: Freeplace infinite canvas + 7 view-mode toggle
3.0: Problem: Predefined views and developer-facing terms (slot / edge / visualization) block the user from defining their own worldbuilding in their own words.
3.1: Analyze: Set the direction so the editor tool mirrors the user's vocabulary and starting state directly. The friendly-language label dictionary keeps internal IDs untouched while swapping only the surface terms, lowering the entry barrier without any data model change. Putting Extract-from-text at the MVP entry further softens the blank-state barrier.
3.2: Action: New project seeds were removed so the canvas truly starts blank. Node positions persist verbatim across sessions so users keep their layout across sessions. When a time field is added, the TimeBar auto-detects it. A unified view-mode toggle put seven modes (free placement / relations / timeline / table / curve / matrix / tree) above every canvas, and developer-facing labels (slot / edge / visualization) were replaced with user-facing words like Galpi and Yeoeum. The sidebar was also redesigned from a flat list into a folder tree that groups Galpi, Yeoeum, and items into folders and exposes an 'only show where this item appears' filter.
3.3: Result: The 13-view catalog was reabsorbed as 7 modes on the canvas, friendly-language labels propagated throughout every surface, and the folder-tree sidebar keeps the codex navigable even as it grows thicker.

4.subtitle: MCP Server for External-AI Co-editing
4.caption: A second entry point that opens the same codex
4.0: Problem: When the only editing path is the user clicking around the canvas, users who need repetitive edits or operate under API constraints face a steep collaboration cost. There was no route to delegate codex-level operations such as 'split out the side characters' or 'hide characters who never appear' to an external AI.
4.1: Analyze: Storytect already runs on the extraction/rendering separation principle and the manifest.json-anchored standardized JSON folder contract, so layering one more entry point on top of the same data agreement was straightforward. Because the desktop app watches file changes and re-renders instantly, external-AI edits and direct user edits converge naturally on the same screen. MCP avoids becoming a second system precisely because the folder contract is established first.
4.2: Action: Built an MCP server that scopes a single project folder as its workspace. Exposed most of the desktop-app's editing surface — read tools (read_manifest / list_slots / read_slot / search_entities), item & relation editors (create_entity / update_entity / delete_entity / create_relation / update_relation / delete_relation), and Galpi metadata editors (add_slot / remove_slot / update_slot_meta).
4.3: Result: An external AI can read and edit the standardized JSON folder directly. Users can now pass their manuscript straight into Claude Desktop and call MCP tools to edit the codex without paying API costs.

0.subtitle: Tauri Webview Hijacking dragDrop at the OS Level
0.caption: Web standards are not self-evident inside a desktop shell
0.0: Problem: Sidebar drag-to-reorder broke at some point. It worked fine in the browser, but inside the Tauri desktop shell the HTML5 drag events never fired at all.
0.1: Analyze: Tauri's webview hijacks OS-level drag/drop and routes it to external file-drop handling. That is exactly why the same React HTML5 drag code works in a browser but never reaches the webview inside a desktop shell. Setting dragDropEnabled: false disables that hijacking so the webview receives standard events directly, with the side effect of also disabling external file-drop UX. Since the app does not use external file drop, that side effect carries no real cost, and a one-line config change addresses the cause precisely.
0.2:
Action

	1. Suspected missing event handlers → confirmed handlers were properly attached
	2. Traced webview console logs → confirmed drag events themselves were never being called
	3. Investigated Tauri webview behavior → discovered the webview intercepts drag&drop at the OS level
	4. Applied dragDropEnabled: false in the Tauri config → HTML5 drag events now reach the webview as expected
0.3: Result: A one-line config restored sidebar drag. Logged as a case where web standards stop being self-evident inside a desktop shell.

1.subtitle: vis-timeline → Direct SVG/HTML → Standalone npm Package
1.caption: Three-stage evolution of the timeline library
1.0: Problem: The timeline view was first built on vis-timeline, but the same event kept landing in different rows every time the user zoomed or panned. The library was recomputing row layout against on-screen pixel coordinates on every interaction. The global TimeBar slider had related issues: dragging it made the time axis tremble, and the cursor flickered between adjacent snap positions even when the user held their hand still.
1.1: Analyze: The root cause was that row layout was decided against the current viewport rather than the full dataset. As the screen zoomed or moved, pixel coordinates changed and the library re-ran row-packing each time, shifting the same event onto different rows. Stabilization options (subgroup, stackSubgroups, etc.) were tried but did not behave as expected at runtime. The conclusion was that rows had to be decided once over the full data, with zoom/pan applying only coordinate transforms. Because this conflicts with vis-timeline's core assumption, switching to a direct implementation was the right call.
1.2:
Action

	1. Tried every stabilization option vis-timeline exposed (subgroup, stackSubgroups, etc.) without resolving the issue
	2. Dropped the library and switched to a direct SVG/HTML implementation. Event rows are computed once from the full dataset, and zoom/pan only recomputes screen coordinates
	3. To prevent label overlap, used Canvas2D measureText to measure actual label widths and placed events with a First-Fit algorithm
	4. Migrated the TimeBar slider's calculation from numeric temporal-value space (year×10000 + month×100 + day) to real ms time values. This eliminated invalid dates like 'month=74, day=54' that JS Date silently overflowed into wildly different real moments
	5. When the snap step became finer than one slider-track pixel, small hand jitter flipped the cursor between adjacent snap targets. Clamped the snap step to be no smaller than the screen pixel resolution
	6. Reserved fixed widths on the cursor readout and the left/right window labels so date-format changes no longer ripple into the slider width
	7. Extracted the direct implementation into a standalone npm package (@mogiyoon/react-stable-timeline). Storytect consumes it through a thin wrapper
1.3: Result: Events now stay on the same row regardless of zoom or pan. The time axis no longer trembles during slider drag, and cursor jumps from hand jitter are gone. Slider squash caused by date-format width changes is also resolved. The vis-timeline dependency was removed, and the stable timeline algorithm itself lives on as an npm package reusable in other projects.

2.subtitle: User-Defined Chart Axes (X / Y axis picker)
2.caption: Mapping Galpi, Galpi values, and time freely onto two axes
2.0: Problem: In chart and matrix views, axes only accepted Galpi (class) categories, which kept users from shaping charts that fit their own worldbuilding. As the dataset grew, the lack of per-chart pan/zoom also blocked visibility into wider ranges.
2.1: Analyze: A visualization tool's freedom is ultimately bounded by what data can be mapped onto which axis. Letting users freely combine three axis sources — Galpi categories, Galpi field values (e.g. a character's age), and time — preserves the data freedom gained from the OO model into the visualization layer. Variant pickers that switch between line / bar / pie and per-axis pan/zoom belong to the same freedom-expansion arc.
2.2: Action: Introduced an X / Y axis picker into the diagram wizard so users choose which of Galpi, Galpi values, or time goes on each axis. Wired a variant picker that toggles the same data between line / bar / pie shapes. Added per-axis pan/zoom so the horizontal and vertical axes can be scaled and panned independently. Adopted an ms-based scale on the time axis so coordinate semantics align with the global TimeBar.
2.3: Result: Following the OO model rewrite and blank-canvas, this third round of visualization-freedom expansion lets users compare the same dataset across multiple axis combinations and chart shapes.

0.1: Validated a Tauri + Next.js 16 + Rust desktop app stack with single-binary builds
0.2: Built an automatic Rust ↔ TypeScript type generation pipeline using specta + tauri-specta
0.3: Designed a standardized JSON interface with the extraction skill that survived two visualization-layer rewrites (Showcase → OO → Blank Canvas)
0.4: Implemented the friendly-language label dictionary (Galpi / Yeoeum), a unified 7-mode view toggle, and a folder-tree sidebar
0.5: Stood up an MCP server that exposes the same codex as a second entry point for external-AI co-editing
0.6: Built and operated two custom multi-agent orchestrators across the planning and implementation phases

1.1: Keeping each planning document separate (brief.md, PLANNING_OO.md, PLANNING_REVISE.md) preserved the assumptions from before and after each rewrite. The structural benefit of being able to trace decisions across both sides of every rewrite became tangible.
1.2: Confirmed that well-abstracted decisions survive rewrites. The global TimeBar abstraction and the extraction/rendering separation principle held intact across two full visualization rewrites.
1.3: Learned the value of expanding user authority gradually. From showcase form to user-defined OO to a blank free editor, each rewrite gave the user more authority over the shape of the tool itself.

features.name: 📂 Instant JSON Visualization
features.description: Renders any standardized JSON folder produced by the extraction skill into wiki, relations graph, and timeline views immediately

features.name: 🧩 User-Defined OO Editor
features.description: Lets users design their own worldbuilding model across four layers: Galpi (class), fields, items, and Yeoeum (diagram)

features.name: ⏱️ Global TimeBar
features.description: Adding a time field to any class auto-registers it onto the TimeBar — a single time highlight shared by every view

features.name: 📝 Extract from Text
features.description: Starts from a blank canvas. Sends the manuscript to Claude, extracts items automatically, and lays them out on the canvas

features.name: 🔌 MCP Server
features.description: A second entry point that exposes the standardized JSON folder to an external AI — Claude Desktop reads and edits the same codex side by side

developmentProcess.title: Planning and Architecture
developmentProcess.content: Defined the extraction-rendering separation principle and the manifest-based standardized JSON interface. Three planning documents kept separate: brief.md → PLANNING_OO.md → PLANNING_REVISE.md

developmentProcess.title: Feature Implementation
developmentProcess.content: Built the 13-view showcase on the Tauri + Next.js stack, evolved into the OO 4-layer model with class_meta, then redesigned around a blank canvas with freeplace + a 7-mode view toggle + global ⌘Z split across 5 parts + a folder-tree sidebar, and added an MCP server entry point on top of the same standardized JSON folder for external-AI co-editing

developmentProcess.title: AI Collaboration
developmentProcess.content: Designed and operated a planning orchestrator (8 agents, parallel fan-out + Critic convergence loop) and an implementation orchestrator (7 stages, Worker → Validator → Fixer sequential pipeline). 195 of 219 commits were Co-Authored-By Claude

claudeInfo.summary: Built with two multi-agent orchestrators driving planning and implementation
claudeInfo.details: Used Claude Code's Agent tool to build a planning orchestrator (8 agents, parallel fan-out + Critic convergence loop) that turned brief.md into a 6-document output package, and an implementation orchestrator (7 stages, Stage Planner + Worker + Validator + Fixer sequential pipeline) that consumed those outputs and drove the codebase through stage-gated build and test checks. 195 of 219 commits ended up Co-Authored-By Claude. The orchestrator definitions themselves were designed in advance using the orchestrator-designer skill.
